title: else Statement
description: The catch-all fallback branch: what runs when every condition in the chain was false.
objectives: Add a fallback else branch to an if / else if chain
Understand that else has no condition of its own
Combine if, else if, and else into a complete decision chain
Know that else is optional and always comes last

sections.text: With marks = 40, none of these conditions are true — nothing prints at all. The chain has no branch for this case. That gap is exactly what else fills.
else defines a fallback block that runs only when every preceding condition in the if / else if chain was false. It takes no condition of its own.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: marks >= 90 → false, marks >= 80 → false, marks >= 70 → false
None of the conditions matched, so the else block runs

sections.text: This is a compilation error. else never has parentheses or a condition — by definition, it only runs when every earlier condition in the chain has already failed, so there's nothing left to check.
5. else is Optional, and Always Last

points: A chain can be just if — no else if, no else
A chain can be if / else — no else if in between
A chain can be if / else if / else if / ... / else — as many else ifs as needed, with one final else
else, if present, must be the last part of the chain — nothing can come after it

sections.text: 6. Simple if / else (No else if Needed)
For a simple two-way decision, if / else alone is enough — else if is only needed when there are more than two possible outcomes.

sections.title: The default option
sections.text: Think of a restaurant menu with special offers listed first: if it's your birthday, get dessert free; else if you have a loyalty card, get 10% off; else, pay full price. else is that last line — the plain default that applies to everyone who didn't qualify for anything special above it.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing else (condition) — else never takes a condition; only if and else if do.
Placing else before an else if in the chain — else must always come last.
Forgetting else entirely when a case should have a fallback, leaving some inputs producing no output at all.
Using an unnecessary else if true) as a fake else — just use else directly.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does the else block do?
questions.answer: It runs as a fallback whenever every condition earlier in the if / else if chain evaluated to false.

questions.question: Does else take a condition?
questions.answer: No — else has no condition of its own; it simply catches whatever wasn't matched by the preceding if / else if branches.

questions.question: Is else required in an if statement?
questions.answer: No, it's optional. A chain can be just if, or if / else, or include multiple else if branches with or without a final else.

questions.question: Where must else appear in a chain?
questions.answer: Always last — nothing can follow an else in the same if / else if / else chain.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: else defines the fallback branch of an if / else if chain, running only when none of the preceding conditions were true. Unlike if and else if, it takes no condition of its own — by the time execution reaches else, every earlier check has already failed. else is optional, and when used, it must always be the last part of the chain. A simple two-outcome decision often just needs if / else, while else if is added when there are more than two possible paths.

sections.title: Trick to Remember else
sections.text: else = the default option. No condition needed — it's what's left when everything else already failed.
